method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Hashima Island (端島), literally meaning "border island," is located approximately 15 kilometers from the center of Nagasaki, Japan.[2] The island is more commonly known by its nickname Gunkanjima (軍艦島), which translates to "Battleship Island" in English.[2]

The name Gunkanjima derives from the Japanese words *gunkan*, meaning "warship," and *jima*, meaning "island."[2] The island acquired this nickname because, when viewed from a distance, its silhouette of high seawalls and densely packed concrete buildings was thought to resemble a battleship—specifically the Japanese battleship Tosa.[2]

The comparison between Hashima and a warship first appeared in print in 1916, when the Osaka Asahi Shimbun reported that the island resembled a giant battleship with two chimneys.[4] The name Gunkanjima itself was subsequently coined in 1921 by the Nagasaki Nichinichi Shimbun, which noted the island's resemblance to the Battleship Tosa.[4]

==History==
Hashima Island's history spans more than a century of industrial development, reflecting broader shifts in Japan's energy policies from the Meiji Period through the postwar era.[2] Within a compressed area of engineered land, the island condensed a century of industrial capitalism, imperial expansion, wartime mobilization, postwar prosperity, and postindustrial decline.[4]

## Early coal discovery and development

According to local legend, people living on Takashima, a larger island near Hashima, had for centuries collected coal from exposed seams and used it as a household fuel, calling it *goheita* after a man who reputedly discovered the rock's combustible properties by accidentally lighting a bonfire on it.[2] Hashima itself began as a small outcrop periodically used by local fishermen and for small-scale coal mining before being gradually transformed by landfill and reinforced concrete construction into a fortified industrial site.[1] The island was expanded by landfill, protected by seawalls, and governed as a Mitsubishi company town, with its built environment functioning as a technology of concentration that organized labor, housing, provisioning, and social life within hard boundaries.[4]

## Mitsubishi era and industrial expansion

By the early 20th century, Hashima had become a major sub-sea coal mining facility operated by Mitsubishi.[3] Concrete apartment blocks, schools, hospitals, and entertainment facilities were constructed to accommodate the miners drawn to the island by the promise of steady work and community life.[5] In 1916, with Hashima producing roughly 150,000 tons of coal annually and its population exceeding 3,000, Mitsubishi built a reinforced concrete apartment block on the island to alleviate the housing shortage and to resist typhoon damage; this was Japan's first concrete building of any significant size, erected only 14 years after America's first large-scale concrete structure, the Ingalls Office Building in Cincinnati.[2] The combination of technology and experimentation allowed for a remarkable number of different buildings to be erected on the confined site, which was repeatedly battered by typhoons, rogue waves, fires, and violent winds.[1]

## Wartime labor

Hashima functioned as one node within a far larger imperial labor regime whose coercive force operated through law, recruitment channels, policing, confinement, wage discipline, and radically unequal exit options, with its effects distributed unevenly across bodies marked by colonial status.[4] The island stands today as a haunting monument to forced labor as well as to rapid modernization and resource extraction.[3]

## Postwar peak

By the 1950s, Hashima had reached its zenith, with over 5,000 residents living in a space of just 16 acres, making it one of the most densely populated places on Earth.[5] At its peak in 1959, the population density reached approximately 83,500 people per square kilometer, and in the residential district the figure was closer to 139,100 per square kilometer.[3] The postwar decades generated an archive of affect—photographs of schools and festivals, stories of children playing on rooftops, and memories of dense neighborliness—that would later be narrated sincerely but often without reference to the coercive histories embedded in the mine's earlier labor regimes.[4]

## Decline and abandonment

The success of Gunkanjima was closely tied to Japan's coal industry, which began to decline in the 1960s as petroleum became the dominant energy source.[5] In 1974, Mitsubishi Corporation closed the mines, and Hashima was abandoned almost overnight, with residents leaving behind apartment blocks, schools, shops, and playgrounds to the mercy of the elements.[5] For decades, the island was left untouched, becoming a ghost town frozen in time as nature began reclaiming it and the buildings started to crumble, earning Hashima a reputation as one of Japan's most haunted and mysterious places.[5]

## Heritage designation and controversy

Hashima was later inscribed as part of the UNESCO listing "Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining" (Decision 39 COM 8B.14).[3] The inscription has been a source of ongoing diplomatic tension, with debate continuing over the possibility of cancelling the nomination.[1]

==Current status==
Since its abandonment in 1974, Hashima Island has remained uninhabited, with its concrete apartment blocks, seawalls, and shrine left to deteriorate under the effects of wind and salt spray.[5] The island lies among 505 uninhabited islands in Nagasaki Prefecture, its barren, treeless crags contrasting sharply with the vegetated peaks of neighboring islands.[5]

## UNESCO status and diplomatic dispute

Hashima Island, inscribed in 2015 as one of the 23 Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution, has remained the subject of an ongoing diplomatic dispute between Japan and South Korea.[1] At the time of inscription, Tokyo pledged to explain the full history of the site, including the conscription of Koreans under wartime labor policies.[1] South Korea has since argued that Japan has failed to honor these commitments, claiming that Tokyo has not properly acknowledged what it calls the forced labor of Koreans at the mine during Japan's colonial rule.[1]

At the 47th session of the UNESCO World Heritage Committee, held in Paris on July 7, 2025, delegates revisited the issue of the Meiji Industrial Revolution sites.[1] The South Korean representative requested that the committee reexamine Japan's follow-up measures and suggested adding the matter to the formal agenda, while Japanese representatives opposed the request, asserting that Japan had faithfully implemented earlier resolutions.[1] With no consensus reached, the committee chair called for an anonymous ballot among the 21 member states, and Japan's revised proposal was adopted by seven votes in favor and three against, with several abstentions.[1] Rather than reopening the issue, the committee endorsed the position that disputes over Hashima Island and its coal mine should be resolved through bilateral dialogue rather than UNESCO procedures.[1]

## Tourism and cultural reception

Hashima Island has become a notable destination for cultural and urban exploration tourism, drawing photographers, historians, and adventure enthusiasts to its ruins.[2] The island's distinctive silhouette and dense concrete architecture, which once housed 5,259 residents within a 16-acre area, continue to define its appeal as a site of industrial heritage.[2] In the contemporary era, Hashima has arguably become better known through its fictional representations than through its archival record, appearing as the lair of the villain Raoul Silva in the James Bond film *Skyfall* (2012) and serving as the basis for survival-horror video games such as *The Closed Ward* (2018) and its imitators.[4] Tourism campaigns and UNESCO heritage designations have likewise circulated images of the island's ruins as picturesque decay.[4]

## Academic and artistic engagement

The island has also attracted academic and artistic attention. The Arts and Humanities Research Council–funded project "The Future of Ruins: Reclaiming Abandonment and Toxicity on Hashima Island," led by principal investigator Carl Lavery with co-investigators Mark Pendleton, Deborah Dixon, and Carina Fearnley, produced a 2013 performance titled *Return to Battleship Island*, combining creative writing and filmed imagery from a visit to the island.[3] A comprehensive 2025 survey of immersive and virtual reality projects focused on Hashima identified eleven projects spanning nearly two decades, ranging from early CAVE-type stereoscopic prototypes to museum installations, university scanning initiatives, community-produced VRChat worlds, and commercial walking simulators.[4] Scholarly analysis has examined how UNESCO inscription, national memory politics, and commercial tourism jointly construct what counts as historically authentic at the site.[4]

==Access==
Public access to Hashima Island is possible only by joining an authorized guided boat tour, most of which depart from Nagasaki Port.[1][2] Nagasaki Port can be reached by a short tram ride of about three minutes from Nagasaki Eki-mae Station toward Sofukuji Station, disembarking at Ohato Station, or by a 40-minute bus ride from Nagasaki Airport to Ohato Station.[1]

Because the island lies in an exposed offshore position roughly 15 kilometers from the city, tour schedules are subject to sea and weather conditions, and departures are never fully guaranteed.[2] Under Nagasaki City regulations, sightseeing boats may only land on the island when wave heights are below 0.5 meters, wind speeds are under 5 meters per second, and visibility exceeds 500 meters, though the captain retains discretion to cancel a landing if conditions are deemed unsafe.[1] As a result of these restrictions, boats are able to land on Hashima on only about 100 days per year, and visitor numbers drop markedly when bad weather prevents berthing.[1][4]

Once ashore, visitors do not roam freely through the apartment blocks or mining structures but are escorted by guides along a short, controlled route focused on exterior viewing of the ruins.[2][4] On busy days, daily visitor numbers can exceed 1,400, with around 260 people passing through per hour, and guides help ease route crowding by directing the flow of tour parties.[4] Straying from the designated viewing routes within the component part of the World Heritage site is not permitted, a rule that has been credited with limiting adverse visitor impact on the Hashima Coal Mine.[4]

Tour narration tends to focus on the daily lives of miners, their families, and community memories as reconstructed through photographs of the abandoned buildings, while the island's wartime role and the history of forced labor are generally not addressed on site.[5]

==In popular culture==
Hashima Island's visibility in popular culture has grown substantially in the twenty-first century, to the extent that it is now arguably better known through its fictional representations than through its documentary record.[2] Its dramatic silhouette of decaying concrete apartment blocks has made it a recurring setting for films, video games, virtual reality projects, and tourism imagery, while also becoming a focal point for debates over historical memory and the ethics of representing sites associated with forced labor.[1][2]

## Film and television

The island gained its widest international audience through the James Bond film *Skyfall* (2012), in which its ruins served as the inspiration for the lair of the villain Raoul Silva.[4] It has also been featured in documentary programming, including National Geographic's *One Strange Rock*, which filmed on the island as part of its exploration of abandoned landscapes reclaimed by nature.[4] Scholars have argued that the proliferation of such imagery has encouraged a triumphalist framing of Japan's industrial past, in which the island circulates as picturesque ruin while its legacy of abuse is displaced by the conventions of mass entertainment.[1]

## Video games and virtual reality

Hashima has been adapted as a setting for survival-horror video games, most notably *The Closed Ward* (2018), which has spawned numerous imitators in the genre.[2] A 2025 survey by Gerteis identified eleven publicly accessible immersive and virtual reality projects focused on the island, ranging from early CAVE-type stereoscopic prototypes and museum installations to university scanning initiatives, community-produced VRChat worlds, and commercial walking simulators.[2] The survey found that these projects consistently confined their representations to either the "golden age" of community life on the island or its present state of ruin, largely avoiding the wartime period of coerced labor.[2] Among the more recent efforts is the HashimaXR project, which uses Unreal Engine 5 to produce rendered reconstructions of the island's residential buildings.[2]

## Memory politics and contested representation

The island's circulation as a pop-culture icon has intersected with ongoing disputes over how its history should be remembered.[1] Media scholars have drawn on visual studies and collective memory research to examine how images of Hashima — including its Google Street View archive — shape navigation, spatial presence, and digital heritage, and how they complicate efforts to hold Japan accountable for past injustices committed on the island.[1] These scholars distinguish between "historical justice" and "collective memory," arguing that Japan's celebration of its industrial sites without recognition of their abusive legacy is reinforced by blockbuster visual campaigns such as *Skyfall*.[1]

In response to such representations, former residents of Hashima, led by Matsumoto Sakae, established the "Society of Hashima Islanders for True History" on January 23, 2017, to contest what they described as distorted accounts of life on the island.[5] In May 2017, the society sent a letter of protest to the *Süddeutsche Zeitung* rejecting comparisons between conditions on Hashima and the Nazi Holocaust.[5] Other commemorative institutions have taken the opposite view: a museum cited by the activist Takazane describes Hashima as a place where "forcibly abducted people lived and worked in a cruel and poor labor environment beyond imagination," noting that workers called the island "hell."[5]

## Tourism imagery

Since its 2015 UNESCO inscription, the island — widely marketed under its nickname Gunkanjima — has rapidly become a major tourist attraction in Nagasaki, with international visitors increasingly helping to spread its reputation.[3] Tourism campaigns and heritage promotion tend to circulate its ruins as images of picturesque decay, a framing that parallels its treatment in fictional and immersive media.[2]

==World Heritage Site approval controversy==
Hashima Island was among 23 industrial facilities included in Japan's bid for UNESCO recognition as "Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution," a designation intended to illustrate the country's rapid transformation from a feudal farming society into an industrial power at the end of the 19th century.[2] The bid's nominated period of focus was 1850 to 1910, which meant that sites related to labor recruitment on the Korean Peninsula under Japanese rule in the 1940s were formally exempted from consideration; nevertheless, Hashima Island—purchased for its coal mines by Mitsubishi Mining in 1890—was included in the nomination.[3]

South Korea objected to the bid, arguing that seven of the 23 locations had used Korean forced labor during World War II and should be dropped from consideration.[1] According to Kim Min-chul, a professor at Kyung Hee University researching the issue, these seven sites were "key to the entire nomination," and Japanese authorities regarded them as "glorious sites."[1] At Hashima in particular, approximately 600 Koreans were reported to have worked deep in the coal mines, with many thought to have died from exposure or overwork.[1] Korean and Japanese diplomats held extended negotiations in Tokyo over the circumstances of the Korean workers in the lead-up to the UNESCO World Heritage Committee meeting in Bonn, Germany, in July 2015.[1][2]

The controversy unfolded alongside broader disputes over Japan's handling of wartime history, including renewed denial that Korean "comfort women" had been coerced as sex slaves by Japanese soldiers, as well as disagreements over Japanese school textbook accounts of imperial rule and wartime conduct.[1] These tensions took on additional significance as Asia marked the 70th anniversary of the end of World War II in the Pacific, placing two key United States allies at odds.[1]

Despite the objections, the UNESCO World Heritage Committee approved the designation of the 23 Meiji industrial sites, including Hashima, in 2015.[5] Critics argued that while the inscription itself was not inherently problematic—pointing to comparable sites such as Auschwitz, the Hiroshima atomic dome, and Liverpool's slave-trade port, which stand as warnings against repeating historical atrocities—the decision to leave Japan and South Korea to resolve questions of classification and description without external adjudication was deeply problematic.[5] Takazane Yasunori, professor emeritus at Nagasaki University and director of the Oka Masaharu Memorial Nagasaki Peace Museum, emerged as a prominent dissenting voice within Japan, arguing that such sites should "provide materials for humanity's self-examination, and warnings of actions that must never be repeated," and that Hashima should be remembered for the correct reasons rather than to celebrate a period of human moral failure.[5]

Six years after the inscription, disputes over how Hashima's history was presented continued.[3] Roughly one week after the Industrial Heritage Information Centre (IHIC) opened to the public in June 2020, South Korean Foreign Minister Kang Kyung-wha sent a letter to UNESCO Director-General Audrey Azoulay arguing that the IHIC's explanations concerning people from the former Korean Peninsula were insufficient, and asked Azoulay to consider removing Hashima Island's world heritage status.[3] Although no action was taken in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, UNESCO subsequently planned to send an inspector to the IHIC following discussions with Japan.[3] IHIC Managing Director Koko Kato, who had gathered more than 100,000 primary materials including testimonies from former residents of Hashima, defended the center's presentation, stating, "I want UNESCO to judge using accurate information. Japan's and South Korea's opinions on the issue are completely different, but Japan is a sovereign state. Japan's sovereignty is a factor when judging history."[3] The center also incorporated work from Japan-based academic Park Kyung-sik, known for disseminating the "forced mobilization" theory regarding Hashima's coal mines in both Japan and South Korea.[3]
